mechanism: Higher marginal rates increase the incentive to shift income to lower-taxed forms, raising the measured behavioral elasticity
Higher top marginal rates increase the progressivity of the tax schedule, increasing post-tax income compression among top earners
Higher statutory CIT rates incentivize multinationals to shift reported profits to lower-tax jurisdictions via transfer pricing and debt allocation
Tax effort captures the gap between potential and actual tax/GDP; higher effort implies higher realized tax revenue relative to structural capacity
Higher voluntary compliance directly reduces the gap between theoretical and actual tax liability
Larger informal sector reduces the taxable base available to the government, depressing tax/GDP ratio
Resource windfalls substitute for tax revenue and weaken fiscal state-building incentives, lowering tax effort
Profit shifting erodes the corporate tax base, reducing CIT revenues as a share of GDP by approximately 1-2 percentage points in high-CIT countries
Higher revenue collection mechanically improves the fiscal balance, ceteris paribus on spending. Long-run balance depends on expenditure dynamics and debt service costs
Persistent deficits accumulate into debt stock via budget identity. The debt-to-GDP ratio evolves as: delta_debt = -primary_balance + (r - g) * debt, where r is interest rate and g is growth

Higher voluntary compliance directly increases realized tax revenue: actual revenue = statutory liability * compliance rate. A 10pp increase in compliance rate increases revenue by roughly the same proportion as a 10% tax rate increase
VAT creates an invoice-matching paper trail that self-enforces compliance through the supply chain without direct tax authority intervention, as demonstrated by Pomeranz (2015)
Countries that mobilize more revenue relative to structural capacity have more fiscal space for productive public expenditure on infrastructure, education, and health
Tax systems relying more on progressive personal income taxes tend to be more progressive overall than those relying on flat-rate consumption or payroll taxes
